sentences.text: Erno Abelesz.
Erno is E-R-N-O.
Abelesz, A-B-E-L-E-S-Z.
Yes.
My name was given to me as Kalman, K-A-L-M-A-N.
No.
The-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the 21st of April, 1933.
That's for Erno.
Because my original birthday is 5th of July, 1930.
I was born in Kapuvar, Hungary.
Kapuvar, K-A-P-U-V-A-R.
Yeah.
This happened in 1949 when I left Hungary illegally, just across the border to Austria.

And at that time, when I was really 19 year old, I thought I will take my deceased younger brother's papers with me, in case if I get caught, I will be regarded as a juvenile and possibly I won't get charged.
And once I arrived in Vienna, I had these papers with me, so I've been registered under that name and date of birth.
And it stick with me right when I came to England.
And I'm known, up to very date, on my younger brother's name and birth date.
Well, Kapuvar is an-- basically an insignificant little place in the northwestern part of Hungary.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The Jewish community constituted about 5% of the total population, which was 10,000.
And so the Jewish population was about 500.
And like people were engaged in the normal little traders, craftsmen, professionals.
But nothing much.
My father was a grocery wholesaler, which was established in the last century by my grandfather.
My mother was-- I mean, we had six children.
She helped in the shop as much as she could.
No.
No.
It was the general community, other shopkeepers.
Yeah.
Very much so.
Very much so.
I mean, most of our customers were from the little villages, little shopkeepers.
They bought their goods from us.
Well, at that time, whatever we regarded as kosher was a basic food, which is-- which don't have to be kosher.
But it was not a specific Jewish shop.
My parents and six children, one girl and five boys.
I was fifth.
I was one but the youngest.
At 1944-- I mean, when we were deported, my oldest brother was 22.
My sister was 21.
And then 19, 16, 14, and 11.
I was 14.
Well, it means that I got-- I got the like cast-over clothes from the older brothers.
That was a disadvantage.
I very rarely got something new.
No.
No.
It's not-- no advantage, because I was not the youngest, I should be called the little favorite.
And I'm not-- nevertheless, I was down on the line.
So I was not-- I couldn't remember any advantage being number five.
Very close.
Very loving.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] With the extended family, I had a great aunt living with us, my grandfather's maiden sister.
And then, at the same time, we had-- we had uncles and aunts, a lot of cousins.
Quite a-- quite a large family.
One of the largest families in our town.
I wouldn't say all, but quite a number of them lived in Kapuvar.
Usually my-- from the paternal side, Abeleszes.
There was-- there was four Abeleszes in Kapuvar besides my father.
I was very naughty.
That's-- that's how I remember.
I was always-- I was very good in school, in the subject of studying.
But I was very misbehaved.
Well, the usual things, scrounging apples from next door and making fun of the teacher when he was not looking.
Sometimes I got caught and then I got a good telling off.
I loved fun and I loved sports.
Generally, I said, a little-- misbehaved little boy can be described.
But I was very good in academical studies.
I really loved books, everything.
I read a lot.
Whatever I could get hold of, I read it.
I've still got this habit.
Jewish life, though we were Orthodox, not extreme Hasidische Orthodox, but-- but very Orthodox.
But the main community was quite a number of with liberal views.
The community itself has been described as Orthodox community.
So we had a Jewish school.
But besides the Jewish school, those-- those children who were more religious, they had their special Jewish instructions.
As a Jewish school, was given very little what we call Hebrew studies, because it was a-- it was a state school.
It was just nominally called Jewish, as the children were all Jewish.
So where did you get your Hebrew instruction from?
Where did that come from?
After-- after and before school, what is called a cheder.
Yes.
Yes.
The synagogue like was a beautiful synagogue, which was given by Count Esterhazy in the last century to the community.
That was the main synagogue where the rabbi and where my parents used to worship.
And there was also a-- in later years, opened up a Hasidische shtiebel.
And the children were more enticed to the-- more to the Hasidische place, because it was more warm.
And so my older brothers, we were more under the influence of the Hasidische, though my parents were strictly kept to the shul, to the main shul.
Oh, there was-- there was a great fight between the two parties, those who were Orthodox and those who are more Orthodox.
In fact, there was-- sometimes in the '30s, there was two separate shochtim-- ritual slaughterers.
One was not satisfied with the others.
And in the later years, it has been already quietened down, especially during the war years.
There were other things to worry about it.
Oh, yes.
I was-- I was-- I remember it as a child.
There was two parties, like almost like two political parties.
One's called the [NON-ENGLISH] and [NON-ENGLISH].
One was a rabbi with a shul and one was a rabbi with a shtiebel.
My family were split-- were split in the middle.
Some belonged-- my-- some belongs to the original one.
Some belongs to the secessionists.
Yes.
I do remember every festival.
I mean, festivals, the Jewish festivals, are the main highlights of the year.
And it was beautiful.
Well, I remember for Passover, when-- when we baked the matzos in the communal bakery.
And in the autumn festivals, when the whole-- everybody came together in the shuls.
And after the last of the day of the festival, where they were throwing sweets and nuts.
And it was January and-- different functions on Hanukkah and Purim.
This was really the highlight what was-- highlights the gray week days.
My childhood-- I mean, I mean, the whole year, school and cheder, quite a-- quite a-- quite a hard regime of studying.
In the summer, there was a long pause of approximately two months where the children, we went to our grandparents, who lived not very far from our town.
That's why we spent a few weeks of the summer in turn with other cousins, because they couldn't cope with all the grandchildren.
That was our holidays.
But otherwise, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] until the-- until the end of the war, I've never left Kapuvar except for these short holidays in the summer.
No.
Not-- not so much socially.
Socially, we were not integrated at all.
I mean-- I mean, this was quite separately.
But-- but as far as respected, or-- the business people in our town, especially my family, were respected by the non-Jewish people as honest-- honest traders.
And the professional people, they were the best doctors, the best solicitors.
And we had quite a good relationship with the non-Jewish community.
Anti-Semitism usually was only from other schoolchildren, from the non-Jewish schoolchildren, sort of Jew-baiting, name calling, which we ignored.
Well, you have to be-- have to be careful, especially-- especially on Easters, when there was all-- from the Catholic church, there was a procession.
And then they said the Jewish people should better stay indoors, because-- because a priest usually talked about it, how-- how their God has been crucified.
And-- and they were-- some of the ignorant persons, they were liable to break some Jewish windows.
But otherwise, I haven't felt-- myself, I never involved an anti-Semitic incident, though there was.
There was.
I heard about it.
No.
It didn't affect me directly.
The childhood generally was quite happy and contented childhood.
My first experience as a child, I remember-- I remember the Anschluss, which was 1938, where Austria was occupied by the Germans.
And really, I remember the worried faces in the synagogue.
And when I asked what-- what went on, then they said that the-- that the Germans had occupied Austria, which is just our next door, considering we were very close to the Austrian border.
And by that time, we knew about Hitler, because that was one of the-- how they were taunting us, the non-Jewish children.
Hitler is coming.
Hitler Is coming.
And-- and I had in childhood a fear that there's somebody horrible, Hitler, who eventually is going to come.
This was-- this was-- this is a point which has overshadowed my happy childhood.
Well, as I-- as I've grown older, I've learned more and more what is going on.
And by-- by certain-- and as per previous years, there was restrictions on Jews.
Then we realized it that we are-- we are second-grade citizens and things are not getting better for the Jews as such.
And the Jewish people were not accepted in the army.
They had to-- they had to cordon in military age into labor battalions under the supervision of the Hungarian army.
And the restrictions on businesses, certain licenses have been taken away, restrictions on the-- on the professions.
We felt it already that we are not safe.
Yeah.
Possibly, it happened before.
But I was-- as a child, I was not aware of it.
But as-- as I've grown up, my awareness has grown, so I realize it, that-- that it's-- it's Hungary is really not our country.
We are here second-class citizens and just hope to pray to God that we will get through the difficult years.
No.
I can't remember that my parents has discussed it with us.
But in school, with other friends, with older children, you get information.
And also, by that time, I was reading the newspapers, so I was quite-- quite well aware what is going on in-- in Hungary and also generally in the world.
I was-- always followed-- followed the world news.
I was really-- perhaps, even-- even-- even at an early age, I was-- I was-- I was involved in world-- current affairs and what's happening in the world.
I remember-- I remember when the war started-- in-- when the Germans occupied France, later the Russians attacked.
The Germans attacked Russia.
And there was war in northern Africa.
I remember I used to make drawings, how the Allied troops stand and where the German troops.
I was very much aware and very much conscious about-- about-- about the war and what's happening around us.
But Hungary itself was like a little island.
We-- we felt that we are safe there, because, especially in our town, even the war has not made much changes in our life.
All kinds of regulations about food.
Certain foods went on rationing.
That was also not [INAUDIBLE] so much an agricultural country.
And what became the refugees, refugees started coming from Poland, Slovakia, and they brought bad news what's happening to the Jews in Poland.
Well, what I remember as a child, they-- they were not talking a lot.

But I realized it that they were sort of saved, saved from a-- from a bad fate, from the general community, the Germans are just killing the Jewish people there and-- we had-- we had even a cousin who escaped from Slovakia who lived in Bratislava.
It was not so much talk, especially in front of the children what is going on, when my uncles have been taken away from Slovakia.
And-- but I-- the fear was already felt.
It was in the air.
And of course, I understood it that it's-- we are in danger.
Hungarian.
Strictly Hungarian.
No.
No Yiddish.
I haven't spoken at home Yiddish, though when I learned at cheder and later on in yeshiva, the language of the instruction was Yiddish.
And gradually, I got to learn the language.
But as not a-- not so much as a living language.
It's more like an academical language for the study of the Talmud.
Always in Hungarian.
Well, you have to believe it.
I mean, it was-- it was-- you could see from the faces of the people who escaped that it's something horrible going on.
And my cousin, who was-- who must have been in his 20s, who escaped, and he knew that his parents had been taken away and he's the only one who managed to get-- to get out.

Little bits of stories and from schoolmates, all the people who-- all the [INAUDIBLE] who were older than me and they came-- those who were already in Budapest, they came down and they-- they were more-- more knowledgeable.
And I was really very aware of it, in fact, more than my schoolmates, somehow, I feel, because I was somehow-- I-- I don't want to brag about that.
I was-- I was really older than my age.
I always felt that I'm-- I'm a-- I'm grown up.
Possibly-- possibly these kind of circumstances which we found ourselves in Hungary.
Possibly they knew about it.
But somehow-- somehow, this kind of subject, it was not discussed.
I mean, we have to pretend.
In order to carry on living, you had to pretend that living from day to day, everything is all right for the time being.
This is a-- this was a half an hour?
Yes.
Yeah.
The schooling went on right-- right almost until the Germans moved into Hungary in 1944.
There was no changes in the-- in the curriculum.
We were still-- still studying the greatness of our country Hungary.
But I realized it by that time, especially when our regular teacher was called in into-- into military service, or rather this labor service, which I mentioned before.
And there was a substitute teacher who taught us.
And he was-- he was really a bit more younger, more friendlier to the children.
And he-- he explained to us that we are not really Hungarians and we are restricted and we were not-- then I realized it, that our-- the culture of Hungary and the fate of Hungary is-- is not us.
Hungary is not us.
We are something different than the Hungarians.
No.
In the earlier years, I was-- I don't know how it worked, but in my mind, I thought this is not contradictory, being Jewish and being Hungarian.
One is complement each other.
I think this is a most wonderful thing to be a Hungarian Jew.
I was very proud of the Hungarian history.
And also about the Jewish history.
And this doesn't contradict each other.
I-- in my childhood, I had-- I thought it a great tragedy what befall our people was that Hungary was-- was taken away territories from Hungary.
Hungary became a [INAUDIBLE] Hungary.
And we were always praying for the resurrection of Great Hungary, which we said it in our prayers.
And another thing, that we are in diaspora as Jews.
But I hadn't found anything contradictory in these two different terms.
Well, it's-- it has not came as a shock, because it's not suddenly it came.
It's somehow-- some-- as one grows older and the situation has changed, then I-- then I gradually, gradually realized the situation.
But it was not, like you put it, a sudden shock.
We learned everything, what normal elementary schools study.
I mean, geography, arithmetics, and what's going on-- world history.
I was quite-- my favorite subject, always, world history and other people.
Geography.
I was very much interested in that.
That was our secular studies.
And parallel, we learned specific Jewish studies as I got grown older.
By the age of 13, I believe, I-- I started studying in yeshiva full time, which was in our town.
Only-- only Jewish subject, I mean Talmud.
Well, the school went up to 14, but somehow I left at 13, because-- because the last class, there was not enough people.
I don't know.
Some of the children went to a-- went to gymnasium, so there was not enough left, enough children left in the elementary school to have the last-- the  14-year-olds.
So I left the school completely.
So I really had education up to the-- up to the age of 13.
They called it just [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Hungarian Autonomic Orthodox State School.
Well, I think-- I think that defines the Orthodox community which had an autonomic existence.
But as far as the subject, we had to-- there was a prescribed by the minister of education what to learn about the general subject-- general secular subject, thrown in a bit of Jewish subjects.
And we had to celebrate, the school, all the national Hungarian holidays, like the 15th of March, when we had to wear something-- colored ribbons.
That's the Independence Day.
And always have the greatest respect to our great leader Horthy.
Even the rabbi came to the school to-- to make a sermon about-- about how significant day it is, the Hungarian independence.
And I realized that by that time, this is something-- nothing to be proud of, of being-- being Hungarian, and we are not really Hungarian.
But-- but you had to pretend.
We were-- we were trying at that time to show to the non-Jewish people that we are-- we are as good Hungarians as they are.
In fact, my father served in the-- in the Austro-Hungarian army in the First World War, and many other adults.
They were good soldiers and good Hungarians and good citizens, as far as being citizens of Hungary.
The school it was-- itself was mixed.
The girls, they usually stayed at home.
Usually, they learned up to-- up to 14.
I think the girls, there was more girls there in the class.
So it was really with the boys who were the problem.
But the girls, they learned up to the age of 14.
And then I think just--
